claim: During air induction melting of Fe-Al alloys, hydrogen gas porosity forms via the reaction of aluminum with water vapor: H2O(v) + 2/3 Al = 1/3 Al2O3 + 2H (dissolved in Al).
evidence_text: The formation of gas porosity in the ingots may be explained by the reaction of highly reactive aluminum with water vapor in the air: H2O (V) + 2/3 Al = 1/3 Al2O3 + 2H (in Al)

claim: For iron-aluminide ingots with a density of 6.7 g/cm^3, only 8 ppm of dissolved hydrogen can result in about 50 cm^3 of hydrogen gas porosity per 100 cm^3 of ingot at STP.
evidence_text: only 8 ppm of hydrogen present in iron-aluminide ingot can result in formation of about 50 cm^3 of hydrogen gas porosity in 100 cm^3 of iron-aluminide ingot at STP.

claim: Addition of carbon to Fe-Al alloys containing 9–16 wt.% Al improves strength, creep resistance, machinability, and resistance to environmental embrittlement, attributed to the formation of perovskite-type Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates.
evidence_text: It has been established that the addition of carbon to Fe-Al alloys containing 9–16 wt.% Al leads to improved strength, creep resistance, machinability, and resistance to environmental embrittlement. These improvements in the properties were attributed to the formation of perovskite-based Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates.

claim: Fe-16Al-1C alloy with Fe3Al phase and Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates exhibits superior high-temperature strength and creep but poor room-temperature ductility, whereas Fe-11Al-1C alloy with bcc α-DO3 ordered Fe3Al and L12 ordered Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates shows good room-temperature ductility, high-temperature strength, and resistance to oxidation and decarburization.
evidence_text: Fe-16Al-1C alloy having Fe3Al phase with Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates exhibit superior high-temperature strength and creep properties as compared with high-carbon Fe-(9–13 wt.%) Al alloys, it has poor room-temperature ductility. Fe-11Al-1C alloy having bcc α-DO3 ordered Fe3Al phases and L12 ordered Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates exhibit an excellent combination of properties such as good room-temperature ductility, high-temperature strength, and high-temperature resistance to oxidation and decarburization.

claim: Reduction in aluminum content enhances room-temperature ductility, which may be attributed to reduced susceptibility to environmental embrittlement and the absence of ordering.
evidence_text: It has been reported that reduction in Al content results in enhanced room-temperature ductility. The improvement in ductility may be attributed to (i) a reduced susceptibility to environmental embrittlement and (ii) an absence of ordering.

claim: In iron-aluminide ingots with a density of 6.7 g/cm³, only 8 ppm of hydrogen can generate about 50 cm³ of hydrogen gas porosity per 100 cm³ of ingot at STP.
evidence_text: only 8 ppm of hydrogen present in iron-aluminide ingot can result in formation of about 50 cm^3 of hydrogen gas porosity in 100 cm3 of iron-aluminide ingot at STP.

claim: VIM was performed in a 50-kg, 125-kW medium-frequency furnace at 10^-4 torr, using an alumina-lined crucible of 150 mm diameter and 300 mm height, pouring at 1893 K into 80-mm-diameter split cast iron molds.
evidence_text: Alloys with the same composition using the same raw material were melted in a 50-kg capacity, 125- kW, medium-frequency chamber vacuum induction furnace from ... with a vacuum level of 10^-4 torr. The furnace consists of an alumina-lined crucible of approximately 150 mm diameter and 300 mm height. ... When the temperature of the melt reaches 1893 K, the melt was poured into 80-mmdiameter split cast iron molds

claim: A cost-effective air induction melting with flux cover (AIMFC) process was developed, using a protective flux containing Al2O3, CaO, MgO, and CaF2 to produce sound, porosity-free ingots of Fe-7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al.
evidence_text: Sound, porosity-free Fe-Al alloys ingots containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al were successfully produced by this process (Fig. 1). ... preheated flux containing Al2O3, CaO, MgO, and CaF2 was added to provide a protective cover to the melt

claim: In AIMFC, 30-kg heats were melted in a 50-kg capacity medium-frequency furnace; iron was pickled, melted in alumina crucible, flux added, cleaned Al plunged, held at 1900 K for 2 min, then top poured into cast iron molds.
evidence_text: The 30-kg heats of Fe-Al alloys containing 7 wt.% and 9 wt.% Al were prepared by Inductotherm medium-frequency AIM of 50-kg capacity. ... The iron charge was cleaned by pickling in dilute hydrochloric acid. ... After melting the iron charge, preheated flux ... was added ... The cleaned and preheated aluminum pieces were then plunged ... The bath was held at 1900 K for a very short time (2 min) ... The melt was then top poured into split cast iron molds.

claim: AIMFC ingots exhibited higher hydrogen concentrations than VIM ingots: Fe-7Al 0.0023 wt.% H (AIMFC) vs 0.0020 wt.% H (VIM); Fe-9Al 0.0052 wt.% H (AIMFC) vs 0.0021 wt.% H (VIM).
evidence_text: AIMFC ingots exhibited a higher concentration of hydrogen compared with VIM ingots (Table I).

claim: Low sulfur in AIMFC ingots (e.g., 0.0016 wt.% for Fe-7Al) is attributed to desulfurization by CaO in the flux via the slag–metal reaction [S] + (CaO) = (CaS) + [O].
evidence_text: AIMFC ingots exhibit low levels of sulfur compared with AIM and VIM ingots (Table I), which may be attributed to the desulfuring action of CaO present in the flux. The sulfur removal takes place by the following slag–metal reaction: [S] + (CaO) = (CaS) + [O]

claim: The AIMFC flux contained approximately 40 wt.% total CaO + MgO with no acid oxide components; the remaining components (CaF2 and Al2O3) were neutral, resulting in low sulfur due to low oxygen activity from high Al and high sulfide capacity.
evidence_text: the major components taking part in the desulfurization reaction are CaO and MgO. The total concentration of CaO and MgO put together is approximately 40 wt.%, while the remaining components in the slag (CaF2 and Al2O3) are neutral.

claim: MgO crucible lining can be reduced by aluminum in the melt at around 1873 K, as shown by standard free energy data (Fig. 2), leading to magnesium pickup and erosion, potentially causing hot working problems.
evidence_text: The standard free energy data (Fig. 2) show that Al in the melt can reduce MgO at temperatures of about 1873 K, which is very close to the melting point of iron aluminides. The reduction of MgO results in the pick-up of magnesium by the alloy and in erosion of the crucible.

claim: Alloys containing higher levels of carbon exhibited significant amounts of precipitates, which were confirmed to be Fe3AlC0.5 by X-ray diffraction analysis (Fig. 6).
evidence_text: On the contrary, alloy containing higher levels of carbon exhibited significant amounts of precipitates. These precipitates were confirmed to be Fe3AlC0.5 by x-ray diffraction analysis (Fig. 6)

claim: The increase in bulk hardness with increasing carbon content suggests that precipitation hardening by hard Fe3AlC0.5 is the predominant strengthening mechanism.
evidence_text: The increase in the bulk hardness values with the increase in carbon (Table V) suggests that precipitation hardening by hard Fe3AlC0.5 is the more predominant strengthening mechanism in these alloys.

claim: Fe-7 wt.% Al alloys are not susceptible to environmental embrittlement, so the decrease in room-temperature ductility with increasing carbon content is solely attributed to the increase in the volume fraction of Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates.
evidence_text: The Fe-Al alloy containing 7 wt.% Al are not susceptible to environmental embrittlement, and the presence of hard and brittle Fe3AlC0.5 precipitate is also normally expected to lower the ductility. Therefore, the decrease in room-temperature ductility in the present study with increase in carbon content may be solely attributed to the increase in the volume fraction of Fe3AlC0.5 precipitates.

claim: A cost-effective ingot metallurgy route comprising air induction melting with a flux cover (AIMFC) has been developed for producing clean and sound Fe-7 wt.% Al ingots, and the properties of alloys produced by AIMFC are comparable to those produced by VIM.
evidence_text: A cost-effective ingot metallurgy route comprising air induction melting with a flux cover for production of clean and sound ingots of Fe-Al alloys containing 7 wt.% has been developed... The properties of the alloy produced by AIMFC and VIM were comparable.
